title: Behind the ATM: Exploring the Structure of Bank Holding Companies

source.name: NY Fed Liberty Street Economics RSS

summary: This post from NY Fed economists Lily Gordon and Lee Seltzer explains the organizational structure of modern financial holding companies (FHCs) and bank holding companies (BHCs), documenting how these structures evolved from 1990 to 2024. The article uses a stylized example (Central Point Corporation) to illustrate how different entities within holding companies are subject to varying regulatory reporting requirements.
key_points: Bank holding companies (BHCs) are regulated under the Bank Holding Company Act of 1956, while financial holding companies (FHCs) were introduced by the Gramm-Leach Bliley Act of 1999, allowing broader financial activities beyond traditional banking
FHCs must derive at least 85 percent of consolidated gross revenues from financial activities to qualify for that designation
Total holding companies declined from 6,307 in 1990 to 3,801 in 2024, reflecting sector consolidation, with BHCs still comprising roughly 80 percent of all regulated holding companies
While FHCs represent less than 20 percent of all holding companies, they hold a disproportionately large share of total assets
SLHCs fell from 459 in 2012 to 190 in 2024, and IHCs number only 11 as of 2024 but account for meaningful asset volumes given parent organization sizes
Different entities within the same holding company file different regulatory forms (FR Y-9C, FR Y-9LP, FFIEC 031, FR Y-11, FR 2314, FR 2886b) depending on their type and jurisdiction
why_it_matters: Understanding bank holding company structure is essential for researchers and policymakers analyzing the financial system. Different entities within holding companies face different regulatory, supervisory, and reporting requirements, which affects how institutions appear in datasets and how their activities are measured. This structural knowledge is foundational for accurate financial system analysis.
market_structure_context: The post directly addresses market structure through its detailed explanation of bank holding company organizational hierarchies, regulatory filing requirements, and the evolution of different holding company types (BHC, FHC, SLHC, IHC) over three decades. It explains how structural differences affect data visibility and research methodology.
important_numbers: 80 percent BHC share of all regulated holding companies
85 percent revenue threshold for FHC qualification

human_review.notes: Source is a NY Fed research blog post from TODAY (2026-03-31), providing foundational educational content on bank holding company structure. No market-moving information; purely structural/regulatory analysis. The source notes the NY Fed is discontinuing the Quarterly Trends for Consolidated U.S. Banking Organizations report and transitioning to periodic blog posts.
